From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Grass field](/w/index.php?title=Grass_field&redirect=no "Grass field"))

Surface on which a sports game takes place

A **pitch** or a **sports ground** is an outdoor **playing area** for various [sports](/wiki/Sport "Sport"). The term *pitch* is most commonly used in [British English](/wiki/British_English "British English"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) while the comparable term in [Australian](/wiki/Australian_English "Australian English"), [American](/wiki/American_English "American English") and [Canadian English](/wiki/Canadian_English "Canadian English") is **playing field** or **sports field**.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

For most sports the official term is **field of play**, although this is not regularly used by those outside refereeing/umpiring circles. The field of play generally includes out-of-bounds areas that a player is likely to enter while playing a match, such as the area beyond the [touchlines](/wiki/Touchline "Touchline") in [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football") or the [sidelines](/wiki/Sidelines "Sidelines") in [American](/wiki/American_football "American football") and [Canadian football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football"), or the "[foul territory](/wiki/Foul_ball "Foul ball")" in [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball").[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

The surface of a pitch is most commonly composed of [sod (grass)](/wiki/Sod "Sod"), but may also be [artificial turf](/wiki/Artificial_turf "Artificial turf"), [sand](/wiki/Sand "Sand"), [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay"), [gravel](/wiki/Gravel "Gravel"), [concrete](/wiki/Concrete "Concrete"), or other materials. A playing field on [ice](/wiki/Ice "Ice") may be referred to as a *rink*, for example an [ice hockey rink](/wiki/Ice_hockey_rink "Ice hockey rink"), although *rink* may also refer to the entire building or, in the sport of [curling](/wiki/Curling "Curling"), to either the building or a particular team.

In the sport of [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), the [cricket pitch](/wiki/Cricket_pitch "Cricket pitch") refers not to the entire [field of play](/wiki/Cricket_field "Cricket field"), but to the section of the field on which batting and bowling take place in the centre of the field. The pitch is prepared differently from the rest of the field, to provide a harder surface for bowling.

A pitch is often a [regulation](/wiki/Regulation_of_sport "Regulation of sport") space, as in an [association football pitch](/wiki/Association_football_pitch "Association football pitch").

The term **level playing field** is also used metaphorically to mean fairness in non-sporting human activities such as business where there are notional winners and losers.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Game court

**Game court** is one of the names for a multi-sport athletic space, typically constructed outdoors, where such games as [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball"), [paddle tennis](/wiki/Paddle_tennis "Paddle tennis") and other racquet sports, and up to a dozen more games and activities can be played. They are usually smaller than a regulation tennis (120' x 60') or basketball (84'x50') court, although there is no set dimensions or size for a game court. The game-court concept was popularized by Sport Court in the 1970s, and some generic references are made to game courts as 'sport courts', although that is a trademark of Connor Sport Court International, LLC. Game courts are often found in residential backyards, giving families and children opportunities for healthy recreation close to home.

Game courts are usually constructed using a rectangular sub-base made from concrete or asphalt, then covered with an open-grid modular [polypropylene](/wiki/Polypropylene "Polypropylene") (or similar) sports surface to improve safety. Most feature athletic equipment such as basketball goals, net systems for racquet sports, volleyball and badminton, lights for nighttime play, fencing or ball containment netting, hockey/soccer goals, lines or markings for various sports, and practice or training components can also be incorporated into the design.

### Surface

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pitch_(sports_field)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Surface")]

The surface of a game court—as opposed to simply playing on concrete or asphalt—is designed for safe play and to reduce injury. Many people have started to use suspended athletic courts to cover old athletic courts like tennis courts and basketball courts. The surface should provide appropriate traction for various types of sports and activities, as well as shock or force reduction to minimize overuse and stress injuries.

Game courts are typically custom-designed to the interests of the family or organization, and are versatile in enabling a wide variety of sports to be played in a relatively small space. Some activities played on a game court are enjoyable modifications of other sports (such as short-court tennis) that allow for similar skills to be developed as the 'regulation' game, but on a reduced-scale court size. A typical game court of 50 by 30 feet (15.2 m × 9.1 m) might include a basketball key and 3-point line arranged around a hoop, overlaid by short-court tennis or [pickleball](/wiki/Pickleball "Pickleball") lines (which can also be used for [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball") or [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton")) along the longer dimension.

### Ball containment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pitch_(sports_field)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ball containment")]

Game courts for private use will frequently be built with a high fence surrounding the surface to allow for containment of the ball used in play, and, if required, to prevent people from entering. Any of several materials have been used, including [chain-link fencing](/wiki/Chain-link_fencing "Chain-link fencing"), [welded wire mesh fence](/wiki/Welded_wire_mesh_fence "Welded wire mesh fence"), and fabric mesh or netting.
